page_text: Red. The pepper is red.
Yellow. The yam is yellow.
Green. The leaf is green.
Brown. The mat is brown.
White. The goat is white.
Black. The pot is black.
Orange. The mango is orange.
All the colours!
Big. The pot is big.
Small. The cup is small.
Big. The yam is big.
Small. The seed is small.
Big. The goat is big.
Small. The snail is small.
A big drum. A small drum.
Big and small!
Naza is at the market.
Mama has a big bag.
The mat is on the ground.
A pot is on the mat.
Naza can see a yam.
The yam is in the bag.
I can see Mama.
Naza and Mama go home.
Ada has a bag.
The bag is red.
A book is in the bag.
A fan is in the bag.
A yam is in the bag!
The bag is big!
I can see it.
Ada and the red bag.
Emeka can run.
He can run on the road.
He can run to the tree.
He can run to the gate.
He can run and run!
Emeka is at the well.
He sat at the well.
Emeka can run!
Ada can see a goat.
The goat is at the gate.
The goat is on the mat!
The goat is in the pot!
No, goat!
Ada and the goat sat.
The goat is a big goat.
Ada and the goat.
Mama has a big pot.
The pot is on the fire.
I can see the pot.
The pot is hot!
Mama sat at the pot.
Naza can see the fire.
Naza sat and sat.
Mama and Naza and the pot.

What do I see?
I see a yam.
What do I see?
I see a mat.
What do I see?
I see a pot.
What do I see?
I see a drum.
I see, I see,
I see it all!

Rain is coming!
Run, run, run!
The mat is wet.
Run, run, run!
The bag is wet.
Run, run, run!
I am wet!
But the rain is good.
Rain, rain, come!
Come on the farm!

Morning is here.
The goat is up.
The hen is up.
The cat is up.
I am up!
Good morning, goat.
Good morning, hen.
Good morning, sun.
Good morning!

book_title: One, Two, Three at the Market
page_text: One. One big yam.

book_title: One, Two, Three at the Market
page_text: Two. Two red peppers.

book_title: One, Two, Three at the Market
page_text: Three. Three green mangoes.

book_title: One, Two, Three at the Market
page_text: Four. Four brown eggs.

book_title: One, Two, Three at the Market
page_text: Five. Five white onions.

book_title: One, Two, Three at the Market
page_text: Six. Six yellow bananas.

book_title: One, Two, Three at the Market
page_text: Seven, eight, nine, ten! So many things at the market!

book_title: One, Two, Three at the Market
page_text: I can count at the market!
In. The yam is in the basket.
On. The cup is on the mat.
Under. The cat is under the table.
Behind. The goat is behind the tree.
In front. Kemi is in front of the door.
Up. The kite is up in the sky.
Down. The ball is down on the ground.
Where is it? Can you find it?
Kemi has her bag. She is going to school.
"Go, Kemi!" said Mama. Kemi ran to the road.
She ran and ran. The school was far.
Kemi saw her friend Ada. "Come!" said Ada.
Ada and Kemi ran to school. They were happy.
The teacher was at the gate. She was big and tall.
"Come in!" said the teacher. Kemi went in.
Kemi sat down. She had her bag. She was ready.
Emeka and Ada were in the yard.
"I can run!" said Emeka. He ran to the gate.
"I can run too!" said Ada. She ran after him.
He ran fast. She ran fast too.
Emeka was at the tree. Ada was at the tree!
They sat under the tree. They were tired.
"You ran fast," said Emeka. Ada smiled.
He was happy. She was happy. They were happy.
Kemi's mother had a fan. It was a big fan.
Mama sat and used her fan. She was hot.
Kemi took the fan. She ran to the yard.
"My fan!" said Mama. "Where is my fan?"
Kemi was in the yard with the fan. She was happy.
Mama came to the yard. She saw Kemi.
"That is my fan," said Mama. She was not angry.
Kemi gave Mama the fan. They sat together.
Amina had a kite. It was her kite.
She ran with her kite. The kite went up!
Her kite was in the sky. It was high up!
The kite went over the tree. Amina ran after it.
The kite was stuck in the tree. Amina stopped.
"My kite!" said Amina. She was sad.
Emeka came. He was tall. He got the kite.
"Thank you!" said Amina. She ran with her kite.
Mama had a mat. It was on the ground.
Rain came! The mat got wet.
"My mat!" said Mama. She ran out.
Naza ran out too. The rain was big.
They got wet. The mat got wet.
Mama and Naza got the mat. They ran in.
Mama was wet. Naza was wet. The mat was wet!
Naza laughed. Mama laughed. It was funny!

Boom, boom, boom!
The drum is singing.
Boom, boom, boom!
My feet are dancing.
Boom, boom, boom!
My hands are clapping.
Boom, boom, boom!
We are all dancing!
Boom, boom, boom!
The drum is singing.
Boom, boom, BOOM!

I know the goat.
She says meh, meh, meh.
I know the hen.
She says cluck, cluck, cluck.
I know the cat.
She says miaow, miaow.
I know them all.
They live in my yard!

We can run!
We can jump!
We can sing!
We can drum!
We can read!
We can write!
We can! We can!
Yes, we can!
One morning, Ada went to the yard. She could not find the red hen.
"Mama!" called Ada. "The hen is gone!" Mama came quickly.
They looked behind the gate. They looked under the tree. No hen.
Ada looked in the kitchen. She looked by the big pot. No hen.
Ada was sad. She sat by the well and thought hard.
Then she heard a sound. Cluck, cluck, cluck! She ran to the garden.
The hen was in the garden, sitting on six brown eggs!
"She has eggs!" Ada called. Mama smiled. The hen was not lost after all.
Bolu wanted a drum. He wanted a big talking drum like Papa's.
"Papa, can I have a drum?" he asked. Papa looked at him.
"Drums are not free," said Papa. "You must earn it."
Bolu went to the market with Papa. He helped carry the bags.
He helped at the stall all day. He was tired but happy.
That evening, Papa came home with something. It was wrapped in cloth.
Bolu opened it. It was a small drum — his own drum!
Bolu sat under the tree and played and played until the sun went down.
In the dry season, Amina went to see her grandma. Grandma lived far away, near the river.
Amina walked along the river road. The tall grass was brown and dry.
She saw a lizard on a red rock. She saw a hawk up in the sky.
The road was long. Amina was tired. She sat under a shea tree to rest.
Then she heard the river. She ran toward the sound. The river was cool and wide.
Grandma was at the riverbank, washing clothes. "Amina!" she cried. She held out her arms.
Amina ran into Grandma's arms. They sat by the river and talked all afternoon.
When the sun was low, they walked home together. Amina held Grandma's hand all the way.
It was the rainy season. Naza went with Papa to the farm.
The farm was wide and green. Naza helped Papa plant yam seeds.
Dark clouds came in fast. "Rain is coming!" said Papa.
The rain fell hard. So Papa and Naza ran to the shed.
They sat in the shed and listened to the rain on the roof.
Naza looked out. The farm was covered in silver rain.
"Rain is good," said Papa. "It feeds the yam." Naza nodded.
When the rain stopped, the farm smelled fresh. Naza and Papa walked home together.
Bisi was the bell monitor at school. Every morning, she rang the bell for assembly.
One morning, Bisi could not find the bell. She looked in her bag. She looked in her desk. No bell.
"No bell!" said Bisi. The children were waiting in the yard. They did not know what to do.
Bisi went to her teacher. "I cannot find the bell," she said. Her teacher was not angry.
"Think, Bisi," said the teacher. "Where did you last have it?" Bisi thought hard.
Bisi ran to the gate. She had left the bell on the gatepost after school yesterday!
Bisi rang the bell. It rang out loud and clear. The children lined up at once.
"Well done, Bisi," said the teacher. Bisi smiled. She would not leave the bell on the gatepost again.

book_title: Why the Tortoise Has a Cracked Shell
page_text: Long ago, Tortoise had a smooth, bright shell. He was very proud of it.

book_title: Why the Tortoise Has a Cracked Shell
page_text: One day, all the birds were going to a feast in the sky. "I want to come!" said Tortoise.

book_title: Why the Tortoise Has a Cracked Shell
page_text: Each bird gave Tortoise one feather. He put them on and flew up with the birds.

book_title: Why the Tortoise Has a Cracked Shell
page_text: At the feast, Tortoise ate and ate. He ate more than anyone. He was greedy.

book_title: Why the Tortoise Has a Cracked Shell
page_text: The birds were angry. They took back their feathers, one by one.

book_title: Why the Tortoise Has a Cracked Shell
page_text: Tortoise was stuck in the sky. He had no feathers to fly home.

book_title: Why the Tortoise Has a Cracked Shell
page_text: He looked down at the earth. Then he jumped. He fell and fell and fell.

book_title: Why the Tortoise Has a Cracked Shell
page_text: Tortoise hit the ground. His shell cracked into many pieces. And that is why, even today, the tortoise carries a cracked shell.
We need water every day. We drink it, cook with it, and wash with it.
Some families get water from a well. They drop a bucket down and pull it up.
Some families get water from a river or stream. They carry it home in pots on their heads.
Some families have a tap in their yard. When the tap is on, the water runs out.
In the dry season, water can be hard to find. People walk far to get it.
In the rainy season, rain fills the streams and wells. Water is everywhere.
We must take care of our water. We must keep it clean.
Water is life. Every drop is important.
Nigeria has many good farmers. They grow food for all of us to eat.
Farmers in the south grow yam and cassava. These foods grow under the ground.
Farmers also grow plantain and palm fruit. These grow on tall trees.
In the north, farmers grow millet and sorghum. These are grains that grow tall in dry land.
Many farmers grow tomatoes and peppers for the market. These are used in Nigerian cooking every day.
Rice is grown in many parts of Nigeria. It is eaten with stew at most meals.
Farmers work hard. They dig the land, plant the seeds, and wait for rain.
When the harvest comes, the whole family helps. It is a time of joy and plenty.

The harmattan is coming.
Dust in the air.
The grass is yellow.
Brown leaves everywhere.
My lips are dry.
My skin is tight.
But the sky is wide and blue
And the stars are bright at night.
Harmattan, harmattan,
Cold in the morning sun.
But the rains will come again
When harmattan is done.

On market day, come!
The road is full of people.
On market day, come!
The stalls are full of colours.
Yam, pepper, onion, palm oil —
All the things we need.
Come and see the market.
Come and hear the noise.
On market day, everything is here.
On market day, come!
It was the day of the school play. Emeka was going to be the narrator. He had practised every day.
"Are you ready?" Ada asked. Emeka shook his head. "I cannot remember the words," he said.
"What do you remember?" said Ada. Emeka thought. "I remember the beginning," he said.
"Then start with what you know," said Ada. "The rest will come." Emeka was not sure.
The play began. Emeka walked onto the stage. All the children were watching.
He opened his mouth. The first words came. Then the next. And the next.
Emeka spoke every word from beginning to end. When he finished, the children clapped and cheered.
"How did you do it?" Ada asked. "I remembered what you said," Emeka told her. "I started with what I knew."
Bolu's drum was gone. He had left it by the tree after school. Now it was not there.
"Where is my drum?" Bolu asked. He looked behind the gate. He looked inside the shed.
He asked Naza. "Have you seen my drum?" Naza shook her head. "I have not seen it," she said.
He asked Amina. "Have you seen my drum?" Amina smiled. "Come with me," she said.
Amina led Bolu to the far side of the yard. There was the drum, under the mango tree.
Sitting on the drum was the goat. The goat was using it as a seat.
"How did the goat move my drum?" Bolu asked. Amina laughed. "I think the goat likes music," she said.
Bolu picked up his drum. He played a long, loud beat. The goat ran away at once.
On the first day of harvest, Amina woke before the sun came up. Baba was already in the yard.
"Come, Amina," said Baba. "Today you will help on the farm for the first time." Amina's heart jumped with excitement.
They walked along the farm road as the sky turned orange. Yusuf walked ahead, carrying the big hoe.
The farm was wide and flat. Rows of millet stood tall, golden, and ready. "This is what we planted," said Baba.
Amina worked with her family all morning. She bundled the millet into neat sheaves while Baba and Yusuf cut.
By midday, they were tired and hungry. Baba's wife had brought tuwo shinkafa and soup wrapped in leaves.
They ate under a tree. Amina was proud. Her hands were strong and her heart was full.
"When I grow up," Amina said, "I will have a farm too." Baba smiled. "You already do," he said.
It was a Saturday and the three friends had nothing to do. "Come to the river," said Naza. "I know a good place."
They walked along the road out of the estate. The sun was not yet hot. Birds were singing in the trees.
Naza led them down a path between tall grass. The path was narrow and the grass brushed their arms.
Then they heard it. A rushing, bubbling sound. "The river!" said Emeka. He started to run.
The river was wide and brown and fast. Rocks stood up from the water. "Do not go in," said Naza. "Just watch."
They sat on the bank. They saw a kingfisher dart from a branch and dive into the water.
"I did not know this was here," said Ada. "It has always been here," said Naza. "You just have to come and look."
They stayed until the sun was high. Then they walked home slowly, still talking about the kingfisher and the river.
Ada and her new friend Stella went to the river one afternoon. Stella knew the river well.
"Look," said Stella, pointing to a rock. Under the rock was a large brown crab, its claws raised.
"How do we catch it?" Ada asked. Stella shook her head. "We do not catch it," she said. "We just look."
Ada lay flat on the riverbank and looked closely. The crab had eight legs and two strong claws.
"What does it eat?" Ada asked. "It eats small fish and river plants," said Stella. "It is an important part of the river."
They watched the crab for a long time. Then it moved sideways under the rock and was gone.
"I want to write about it," said Ada. "I will draw it in my book tonight."
That evening, Ada drew the crab with all eight legs. She wrote underneath: This is a crab. It lives in the river. It is important.

book_title: How Anansi Got All the Stories
page_text: Long ago, all the stories in the world belonged to Nyame, the Sky God. He kept them locked away. Anansi the spider wanted those stories more than anything.

book_title: How Anansi Got All the Stories
page_text: Anansi climbed up his web to the sky and asked Nyame for the stories. Nyame laughed. "What can a small spider give me for all the stories of the world?"

book_title: How Anansi Got All the Stories
page_text: "Name your price," said Anansi. Nyame smiled. "Bring me the python, the hornets, and the leopard," he said.

book_title: How Anansi Got All the Stories
page_text: For the python, Anansi said, "People say you cannot stretch as long as this bamboo stick." The python stretched to prove him wrong. Anansi tied him to the stick with spider thread.

book_title: How Anansi Got All the Stories
page_text: For the hornets, Anansi poured water on them. "Fly into my gourd to stay dry!" he cried. They flew in. He stopped up the gourd.

book_title: How Anansi Got All the Stories
page_text: For the leopard, Anansi dug a pit and covered it with sticks. The leopard fell in. Anansi helped him out — but tied his legs while doing so.

book_title: How Anansi Got All the Stories
page_text: Anansi brought all three to Nyame. The Sky God shook his head in wonder. "How did you do this?" he asked.

book_title: How Anansi Got All the Stories
page_text: Nyame gave Anansi the golden box. From that day, all the stories in the world have belonged to Anansi — and so to all of us.
Jollof rice is one of Nigeria's most loved foods. It is cooked at celebrations and eaten every day in many homes.
To make jollof, you need rice, tomatoes, peppers, onions, palm oil, and seasoning. These are the things that go into a good pot of jollof.
First, blend the tomatoes, peppers, and onions together until they are smooth. This is the tomato base.
Next, heat the palm oil in a big pot. Add the tomato base and fry it, stirring, until the raw smell is gone.
Then add water and seasoning. Let it boil. Taste it and adjust the seasoning.
Now wash the rice and add it to the pot. Put the lid on. Lower the heat and leave it.
When the rice is tender and all the water has been absorbed, the jollof is ready. The bottom of the pot will be slightly browned — this is the best part.
Serve the jollof with fried plantain, chicken, or fish. Eat with your family and enjoy.
In cities and towns across Nigeria, you will see a small three-wheeled vehicle. It is called a keke napep, or keke for short.
The keke has three wheels, a small engine at the back, and a covered seat where passengers sit. The driver sits at the front and steers with handlebars.
Keke napeps were first introduced to Nigeria in the early 2000s. Today, they are one of the most common forms of transport in many Nigerian cities.
A keke can carry two or three passengers at a time. It is faster than walking and cheaper than a car.
Keke drivers know their neighbourhoods well. They know every road, every shortcut, and every market.
Keke napeps are important for people who need to travel short distances — from home to the market, from school to the house, from the clinic to the church.
Some keke drivers are women. In recent years, more and more women have become keke drivers in Nigerian cities.
The next time you see a keke, look at how it moves through traffic — quick, careful, and always busy.

I like the smell of rain on dry ground.
I like the sound the talking drum makes.
I like the taste of cold ogi in the morning.
I like the feel of warm sand between my toes.
I like the sight of the market all lit up.
I like the way the mango tree looks at sunset.
I like the songs my grandmother sings.
I like to read.
I like to learn.
I like being here,
in this place,
in this time,
with these people.
This is what I like.

Every journey starts with one step.
Tashi — the beginning.
A step, a stumble, a step.
Mataki — you are moving.
The road gets longer.
The sky gets wider.
Hanya — the path is opening.
You know more than you knew.
Tafiya — you are on the journey now.
Keep going.
Keep reading.
Keep asking questions.
Every word is a step.
Every book is a road.
The journey never ends.
Go.
